skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python code through the inference.sh sandbox using the belt CLI. The skill supports data analysis, scraping, media processing, document generation, API calls, and automation with many pre-installed Python libraries.
skill.summary: Run Python scripts in a remote sandbox with common data, web, media, and automation libraries.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick findings, but inspection shows Markdown code formatting and command examples, not hidden Ruby execution. The skill intentionally runs user-supplied Python through the third-party belt CLI and documents network-capable libraries, so it has medium operational risk. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, or a real weak cryptographic algorithm.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Remote Python Code Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access to the belt CLI and shows belt app run commands that send user-supplied Python code to infsh/python-executor. This matches the skill purpose, but it is still arbitrary code execution in a third-party sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and command examples directly show user-supplied Python sent to a remote executor. The context confirms an intended capability rather than hidden malware.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Scraping and API Execution
medium_findings.description: The documented environment includes HTTP clients, scraping libraries, and browser automation tools, with examples that fetch external websites and APIs. This is legitimate for the skill, but users could send sensitive data or perform unauthorized scraping.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly lists network libraries and provides HTTP request examples. The risk depends on user input and target sites, so it is elevated but not malicious by itself.

low_findings.title: Third-Party Execution Service Dependency
low_findings.description: The skill relies on inference.sh and the belt CLI, with external install and documentation links. No malicious endpoint is shown, but users must trust the third-party service with code, inputs, and generated outputs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The service dependency and external links are explicit. There is no evidence that the URLs are malicious, so this is a trust and data-handling concern.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most reported Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code spans, fenced examples, package names, or command documentation. The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at line 3 has no supporting cryptographic context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Inspection shows Markdown documentation and code examples, not Ruby backtick execution. No cryptographic operation is present at the cited weak-algorithm location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access Limited to belt CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The front matter allows Bash commands for belt. This limits the exposed command surface, but it still permits remote execution workflows through that CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry is explicit and narrowly scoped to belt. The downstream risk comes from what belt runs remotely.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied code Field Sent to Executor
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples document a code input field used to submit Python source to the remote executor. This is expected behavior, but it is the primary hazardous capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The input schema and quick start both show a code field containing Python source. There is no ambiguity about the execution model.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executed Python Can Make HTTP Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples call external URLs from within executed Python code. This can be useful, but it creates data exposure and policy compliance risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code examples explicitly use requests.get against public URLs. The observed behavior is network access, not local-only computation.

content.user_title: Run Python Code in a Remote Sandbox
content.value_statement: AI coding agents often need Python execution for data, media, and automation tasks without local setup. This skill routes Python scripts through inference.sh with common libraries and returned output files.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through infsh/python-executor using the belt CLI.
Supports common data libraries such as NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly.
Supports HTTP, scraping, and browser automation libraries listed in the skill.
Creates output files in outputs/ for charts, CSV files, videos, PDFs, and 3D assets.
Offers default and high-memory variants with documented RAM and timeout settings.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and authentication with inference.sh.
Runs CPU-only workloads and does not provide GPU or ML model execution.
Code is non-interactive, so visual output must be saved to files.
Arbitrary Python execution can expose sensitive data if users include secrets.

use_cases.title: Analyze and Visualize Data
use_cases.description: Run Pandas workflows, summarize datasets, and return saved charts or CSV files.

use_cases.title: Automate Web Data Collection
use_cases.description: Use HTTP clients and scraping libraries to collect public data for review or processing.

use_cases.title: Generate Media and Documents
use_cases.description: Create images, videos, PDFs, and 3D files from scripted Python workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation of the execution environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints library versions for pandas and matplotlib. Keep the code read-only.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Dataset
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate data processing with a saved chart.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to load the dataset I provide, compute summary statistics, and save one clear chart in outputs/.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Visual Assets
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced media generation from Python libraries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to create a branded image asset with Pillow. Save the final PNG in outputs/ and summarize the design choices.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-step Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with network access and generated artifacts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch public API data, transform it into a table, create a chart, and return both files from outputs/.

output_examples.input: Analyze a small sales table and create a chart.
output_examples.output: A concise sales summary, a returned bar chart image, and notes about the highest and lowest values.

output_examples.input: Create a simple PDF report from provided metrics.
output_examples.output: A returned PDF report with a title, metric table, and short explanatory notes.

output_examples.input: Fetch public profile data from an API and summarize it.
output_examples.output: A short text summary and a returned CSV file containing selected public fields.

best_practices: Review generated Python before execution, especially network and file operations.
Keep secrets out of scripts and inputs sent to remote services.
Set explicit timeouts and save required artifacts under outputs/.
anti_patterns: Do not run untrusted code without reading it first.
Do not use it for GPU training or long-running interactive sessions.
Do not scrape sites that prohibit automated access or require unauthorized credentials.

faq.question: What does this skill execute?
faq.answer: It executes Python code through the infsh/python-executor app using the belt CLI.

faq.question: Does it run code on my local machine?
faq.answer: The documented workflow sends code to inference.sh for sandboxed execution rather than local Python execution.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The examples and listed libraries show HTTP requests, web scraping, and browser automation support.

faq.question: Can it return generated files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved under outputs/ are documented as returned artifacts.

faq.question: Is it suitable for sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Do not send secrets or private data unless your organization approves the third-party execution service.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it intentionally enables arbitrary Python execution with network and file output capabilities.
